Yes, it is possible to use a different `create_app` function when running your Flask application as a server versus when running CLI commands. You can achieve this without needing to set an environment variable by leveraging the `app` object that is passed to the CLI command. The `create_app` function can be called with different parameters based on the context in which it is used.

In the app factory pattern, the `create_app` function is typically called to initialize the Flask application. When you run the Flask server, the `create_app` function is called to generate the application instance, and this instance is then used to run the server. When you run CLI commands, you can pass the `app` object to the CLI command, which allows you to customize the application based on the context.

One way to achieve this is by using the `app` object that is passed to the CLI command. For example, you can define a CLI command that takes the `app` object as a parameter, and then use it to configure the application. This allows you to differentiate between the server and CLI contexts without needing to set environment variables.

You can also pass parameters to `create_app` when running a CLI command. For instance, you can pass a flag or a configuration parameter that tells the `create_app` function to use a different configuration or to load a different set of routes. This can be done by passing a keyword argument to `create_app`, such as `config` or `app`.

The key to this approach is that the `create_app` function is called in a way that allows it to be customized based on the context in which it is used. Whether you're running the server or a CLI command, the `create_app` function can be called with different parameters to reflect the context.

In addition to passing parameters to `create_app`, you can also use the `app` object to access the application instance and modify it as needed. This allows you to set up different configurations or routes based on the context in which the application is being used.

To make this more flexible, you can define a CLI command that accepts the `app` object as a parameter. This allows you to run the CLI command with a different application instance, which can be used to set up different configurations or routes.

You can also use the `app` object to access the application instance and modify it as needed. This allows you to set up different configurations or routes based on the context in which the application is being used.
